narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and your choice to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 55.5% chance that you might fail it—slightly above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was in the bottom tenth of your peers, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam struggle compared to students who ranked much higher on average. Your undergraduate GPA, sitting at 2.90 below the typical 3.21, also nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, though less forcefully. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 37—right above the dataset average—had the second-strongest influence, pulling the odds meaningfully in favor of passing and showing your potential in that area. Your Year 1 decile of 3 and middle family income bracket played smaller roles in tipping toward failure, while your full-time status offered a minor boost toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students. Overall, your profile stands out as having a weaker late-stage academic record than typical admitted students, who often maintain higher deciles throughout and stronger GPAs, despite your competitive LSAT. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings and GPA, as those were the top concerns here, and perhaps seek targeted bar prep resources tailored to your background. Reapplying with strengthened academics could shift the model's view dramatically in your favor. We're rooting for you—keep pushing.
